sentences.text: Sala Hudes.
So when we-- when we got there to the little town, was called Kopeisk, which is a few-- I can't remember exactly how many miles from the main city of Cheliabinsk.
At that time I was already pregnant.
I was pregnant.
And they brought us.
And they send us to work in the coal mines.
And it was--
The Russians.
They gave us rooms.
And we were like six people in a room.
And they send us to work in the coal mines.
They gave us cards to rations, you know, for bread, for soup.
And we used to come up from the-- from the coal mines to eat that soup.
I didn't work long, long day.
My husband did.
Then I stop-- I stopped working.
And I used to get just bread, not any-- not anything else.
But the few little things that we had, we used to go to the market and sell it and buy bread, buy a few potatoes.
And so we survived there for a few months, and my-- until probably, maybe July, no, [INAUDIBLE] September because my son was born in Cheliabinsk.
And then we decided that is not for us.
And we got to the train.
I remember we were sitting at the station for about two days, two nights until we got tickets, all of us, all our friends.
And we got on the train.
And we escaped, came back from Russia, came back to town of Lwow, which used to be Poland, but at that time was occupied by the Russians.
It's-- Lwow would be close to Ukraine.
So when we came back to Lwow, we got off the train.
We had no place to go.
So we stretched out on the streets.
And at night, you know, in the daytime, we were sleeping on the street.
People were walking, you know, going over us.
But we didn't care.
We didn't mind.
We didn't feel it.
We didn't have anything.
We didn't need anything.
At that time, there's only one mind-- one-track mind to survive.
And the war was, you know, was no war because the Russian, the Germans made a pact, so there was no war.
So we-- we were there in-- in Lwow.
And we found out that there is a lot of refugees living in a school.
And the school was on, if I could remember, the [INAUDIBLE].
And it's called [INAUDIBLE] School.
And they directed us.
So when we got there, there was hundreds and hundreds of refugees.
So we had a place to stay.
There was in one room maybe 100 people.
There was a lot of straw all around the hall, was nothing but an empty hall.
And we was lying like sardines.
And we went out into the street to look for food.
People that lived there used to give us food.
And so we survived there.
And we find out that they registered people to go home, to Warsaw or wherever you want to go.
So I decided that I want to go back to my parents.
We want to go home.
So we went to register.
We were standing in the line for three days, three nights.
And it came maybe about 30 people before me, they close the window.
They said they're not taking any more.
There's no more place.
So we were really heartbroken.
I cried that I can't go home.
And it turned out that that was my good charm, because would that I go home with the transit, they were straight-- straight to Auschwitz.
Nobody got home.
So that's how we remained.
So we were living there.
When we were living there, the-- one day, the Russian military surrounded the school.
And they took all the young men out to Russia.
They took all-- and they said that the married people with children, they not going to touch.
But sure enough, the same night, they-- they took us too.
All the people, they came back, surrounded the rest of the people that remained there, and took them back to the railroad station, took us into the-- the cattle wagons.
I was six or seven.
I can't remember.
So my husband was standing by the wagons and looking, watching what's going to happen.
Finally, he seen that in the very beginning, they begin to lock, lock up, so people couldn't get out.
So he jumped into the wagon, took the few positions that we had, helped me down, and we escaped.
And we escaped with two other couples.
We got underneath the wagons to the other side and went in-- ran into the woods, because there was everywhere was a lots of trees.
So that night, we escaped, again, because we didn't want to go back to Russia, because we had come back from Russia.
We knew what life was like.
So we didn't want to go.
We wanted to go home.
And we didn't get to go home either.
That night, we find out from somebody that there is in the suburb of Lwow, there's a lot of farms and maybe we could work there.
So we begin to walk.
We begin to walk there.
And was-- I can't remember how many kilometers.
But nightfall came, and it was dark, and we got somehow entangled in the fields that they were digging for tuff.
You know, tuff is like coal, but is, instead of mines, it's dark from wooden.
So it was dark.
We didn't see where we are going.
So my husband said let's stop where we are, because we're going to drown.
We don't know.
And so we did.
And as-- in the middle of the night, one of the ladies was already due, and she start labor.
And there we were.
We were three women, three men.
We didn't know what to do.
I was pregnant myself.
No.
And she was crying.
And he was begging her to wait, as if it would be up to her to wait.
Now, I realize.
But then, it was scary.
Finally, my husband and the other men notice a light far away.
So they walk.
They left us, and they walked towards the light.
And it turned out to be there were the watchmen that were watching the tuff not to be stolen.
And they find out that a few miles, there is a farmer living.
So they came back.
And I remember she could not walk no more.
So my husband and her husband made a stool from their hands and carried her.
When we go to the farmhouse, knocked at the door, and I remember the farmer, he came out in his underwear.
But, of course, he didn't know what happened.
As he opened the door, they just bursted in, all of us, the six of us.
We bursted in and told him what was happening.
And his wife jumped off the bed, and boiled water, and the baby was born.
Right after the baby was born, my husband took off his shirt and wrapped the baby, and we walked down to the village, because all around, there was nobody.
He was by himself.
Miles around was absolutely nothing.
So we came to a village several miles back.
Oh, she walked with us.
He kept-- he hold the baby, and she walked with us.
When we came to the village, it was very early in the morning.
There was one barn was open.
So we opened the barn.
And we all lay down in hay, you know, in the hay.
It was stacked with-- with hay.
And we went to sleep.
Then all of a sudden the door opened, and the owner came in and asked us, what you are doing here?
We said, we escape from the Russian train.
He was very nice.
He gave the other two people a place where to go.
And he took my husband and myself to his house.
He had a big family.
He was a Ukrainian.
He was a disabled man.
He had-- he was paralyzed his right hand and right foot.
My husband told him that he's a carpenter.
He took him to his-- to his house.
He sent his children out to sleep in the barn.
He gave me their bed.
They treated me-- not-- not-- you know, those were the nicest few weeks since I left home that I enjoyed because I had food.
His wife, she milked the cow every morning.
She woke me up to drink fresh milk because I was pregnant, maybe six months.
I can't even remember exactly.
And we were there for a few weeks until one day, he came, and he said, the Russians are searching every house.
And I don't want you to be caught.

They were searching for refugees because if you took a passport, a Russian passport, which they made a paragraph, which is was a special number, we were afraid if we're going to take a passport and if time came-- came to go home, they not going to let us go home.
So we were avoiding to take Russian passport.
So we were hiding.
And then they were searching every house.
So I remember one day, one day he-- he saddle out his horse.
My husband made him a new buggy while we were there.
And he helped him take off everything from the field.
And he was so appreciative.
He didn't know.
He kissed us.
He hugged us.
He didn't know what to do.
He felt so sorry for us.
And that day he came in, he says, I don't want to let you go, but you have to go because they're going to catch you.
He settled out his horse and buggy, took his daughter because he couldn't hold the reins in his hand, and took us to the station in Lwow.
He also gave us a big jug of milk and a bread, about 10 pounds.
No.
I don't remember none of the names.
We-- we knew that in a little town called [NON-ENGLISH].
There is some of our friends that we were previous in Russia.
So we wanted to go there to be with somebody together because we were only by ourself.
So we got to the train.
We bought tickets.
And we went to the little town, to [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was by the railway.
And my husband and the other people that were with us lived there.
They were working for the Russians by the railroad.
It was not far from Sarnow on the way to Kiev.
And we were there.
We-- the place we lived was in the synagogue.
The people didn't want to let us into their house.
So the Russians-- they took care of-- you know, had, like here, the City Hall.
They said, if you don't want to let the refugees into your house, we're going to give them the synagogue.
So that's where we lived, in the synagogue for quite a few weeks until--
No, not at all.
What helped us was the-- the Ukrainian farmers gave us food.
The Jewish community didn't want to help us at all.
They were afraid of us.
They thought that we are all thieves, that as soon as they going to let us into the house, we're going to steal.
And they even didn't let us in, which regrettable, but that what it was.
So we went to the farmers for food.
We lived there until the war broke out between the Germans and-- and the Russians.
That was in June, some times in June.
I can't remember, and I think it was in '41, June '41.
No, no because my baby was born when I returned to Russia.
No, I had the baby because my son was born in '40.
So yeah, I had previously before I left.
He was born in Cheliabinsk in 1940.
See, this was '41.
No, because you see, my son was born in Cheliabinsk.
Then when we run away from Russia, we run with my son.
That's when I was involved with my son.
And that's where was at-- at that Ukrainian with my son.
And he came--
My son's name is Jacob.
And he took a--
When he was born, well, he was not circumcised, because you could not circumcise in-- in-- he was-- yeah, he was circumcised because when, you know, when I lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
In that little town [NON-ENGLISH] there was a rabbi.
So he was circumcised.
It's my youngest son that was not.
He was circumcised.
And then when I got with my son back to Russia, because we didn't want to take the passports, because we were afraid we'd never get home, so when the-- when the war broke out, we came back to Russia.
They-- they brought us back to the very same place from where we escaped before.
When we've got there, of course, my husband went back to work in the coal mines.
And times were so bad.
But we a friend, a Russian Jew.
His name was Luen.
I was pregnant again with my younger son.
And he was so good to us.
He wanted to help us.
So he gave me a job in a diner.
So I used to take my-- my Jacob to the day care.
But the day care consisted there of about 100 children, one person taking care, and a cement floor.
So I was working the diner.
I was stealing food.
And my food, what I was stealing consisted of ears from the hogs.
And I used to come home.
I used--
I steal-- I would put-- put-- I put the ears from the hogs in my panties.
And that's how I used to bring it in the house when I came home.
On the-- you know, the next day, I would go in the-- I would go in the fields.
I would collect edible leaves.
And I had some ears.
And I used to cook soup.
And-- all the time
Well, all your-- your mind is concentrated where to get food.
Nothing matter.
You don't care what you wear.
You don't care where you live.
You don't care where to sleep.
Many places I slept that when I put my head against the wall, I used to take out my head-- again, I used to take away my head and leave my hair by the wall because they were frozen to.
I fed my baby.
I-- I used to pick up snow, melt it to my palm, and that's what I had water.
So when we came back to Russia, back, they gave us a room to live.
And my husband went to work in the-- in this-- they call it [INAUDIBLE], which is--
Oh, we stayed there until-- the second time when I came back to Russia, we stayed there until the end of the war, until '46.
But in the meantime, my son got sick.
He-- he caught a cold.
From cold, he caught pneumonia.
From pneumonia, he caught tuberculosis.
There-- there was doctors.
But there was no medicine.
My Jacob.
There was no medicine.
There was no-- no nothing, was cold.

Many times, I used to go with other women in the-- at night, and we used-- when somebody said that there is a transport of coal on the railroad, you know, those cattle wagons with coal, so we used to go, several for us, like five, six.
One would stay watch.
The rest would climb up, put maybe 100, 150 pounds of coal in it each sack.
And we would fill the sack for the woman that was standing watch, because the transit sometimes consists of about 60, 70 cattle wagons, which was a very big line.
She would watch.
And if she saw the watchman, because he used to have a flashlight, if she saw him from far away coming, she would whistle.
So we would throw down the sack with coals, grab it on our back, and run home, so we had something to--
My second son was born in '42.
His name is Aron.
Yes, because you see, my Aron was raised at the same circumstances.
But he never got sick.
He-- it's-- it's a different story.
But when my Jacob got sick, and, of course, he got tuberculosis, and was nothing to heal him.
[SIGHS] He was running around when he was two.
He stopped walking when he got sick.
And I-- I don't know.
Probably, it's the will of God because I watched him die.
Like one day I remember he turned blue.
He had a foam in front of his mouth.
And I dripped water into his mouth, and it came out.
He didn't-- he couldn't swallow.
So I was screaming.
And across the hall was-- lived a doctor.
She was a lady, a Russian lady.
She came in.
And she took me around.
She hugged me.
And she says, I feel so sorry for you and so sorry for your son.
But there's nothing we can do.
And she-- she says, I can really help you.
There was really nothing.
And I said, no.
He's not going to die, because if there is a will of God, he's going to live.
And she left me.
And I kept-- I kept melting snow in my palm, and I dripped in his mouth.
And I really watched him die for several hours.
All of a sudden, I notice that he swallowed a drip, you know, of water, and another one, and another one.
And little by little, his color changed.
And he-- he recovered.
My husband talked to our friend that gave me work in the diner.
So he took him out from the coal mine and he put him to deliver groceries.
And, of course, all your mind was how to survive.
So my husband, he would tie his underwear and put some sugar in, in one, put something else in the other.
And that's how I brought my son back--
--from--
No.
There was no-- I never knew if anybody's alive.
I had lost contact because there was no mail.
It was war.
The Russian and the-- and the Germans and the Russian was-- the Germans were coming closer and closer until Stalingrad.
That's when they stopped.
And that's when they begin to go back.
And I was far away.
I was thousands of miles from there.
The end of the war came in May of 1945.
But we didn't come back right away.
I-- we came back.
I-- I can't remember the reason why.
It took probably time until things will settle.
And we came back in 1946, in the spring.
Oh, we felt relief.
And everybody knew that we're coming back and we're going to meet our family.
There was-- there was a lot of us there that survived.
The-- I-- only I could say the Russian people as a whole were very, you know, they were very compassionate.
They tried to help.
But they were in the same boots that we are.
They hadn't-- they had no food.
They had no coal.
They-- all of them went on the front.
I-- well, that was a coincidence.
When we came-- when we came home, home-- when we came to Silesia, when they brought us--
I can't remember the date.
That was in the early spring of '46.
When the-- when the transit came and it stopped in each place in Poland, there was thousands of people at the stations, because everybody was looking for somebody that came from Russia or survived.
So we came to a little town that used to be a taken by the-- the Germans used to-- they used to call them the false Deutsche used to live there.
And it was called Reichenbach.
Yes, that's when.
They would change it later to Dzierzoniow.

So when the train stopped there, because we had no idea where they're taking us, when the train stopped and opened the door from the wagons, my husband saw, you know, his friends that he lived with in when he would race with him, grew up with him together.
When they saw us, they ran into-- they jumped into the wagon, took-- took the kids, and took the few, you know, schmattas that we had, and said, you come and live with us.
They already had a-- a room.
Up till they had like a room and a little kitchen because they-- they evacuated the-- the Russians evacuated all the Germans, and they gave us their homes.
Probably, a couple months later.

It was by sheer coincidence, because one of my husband's friends went back home where he lived, because my husband's brother returned from Russia on-- from Arkhangelsk, which is close by the Chinese border.
And he found out my husband has another brother that lived in, at that time, in New York.
And he found out that he send him money.
And he send money to share with anyone from the family that survived.
So when he came back, he told me that if I want to get some money, I should go to Ciechanow, where my husband is from, and get the money.
And that happened.
There were-- that happened just after Kielce pogrom that used to be in Poland.
And that time was about 60 people killed by the underground army that were fighting the Russians.
And it was very dangerous for men to travel.
So I decided not to let my husband go.
I left my husband with two children.
And I said, I'm going to go.
And--
I'm going to-- I'm going to-- no, I had no idea that anybody from the family lived.
I was going.
I already knew my husband's brother came, which he was only-- he was my brother-in-law.
And he was all on-- also my cousin.
So I went to get-- actually, I went to go see them and get the money, get some money.
At that time, like $50 was a fortune.
So I embarked to-- to--to-- to go.
I was staying in the train like a sardines smashed.
And there was a guy, a Polish guy, I remember that he had a little-- a box he was sitting on.
And I-- I-- I managed to Polish.
I speak Polish very well.
So he strike up a conversation with me.
And in the meantime, he told me to sit down on the box.
And all night long, he was telling me how many Jews he killed.
And if he would have the power, he would kill more of them because look how many survived and look how many came back from Russia.
And I didn't say nothing.
I always say, you're right.
You're right.
You're right, until the morning when the train stopped in Warsaw.
And we got off the train.
I told him that I am Jewish.
And if I had something to do, if I had the power, he wouldn't be here too.
And then I parted.
And when I parted, and I had to-- I had to lay over.
I had to wait for another train.
And finally, I arrived to Ciechanow.
And I got off the train.
And I start walking.
Finally, I put my head down, and I saw Jewish inscriptions.
And I realize that I'm walking on gravestones.
I right away got off the sidewalk.
And I walk on the road, came to my brother-in-law.
And he was--
No.
I was in Ciechanow.
Then I had there a-- a guy.
He was a gentile guy that grew up with my husband, and he was peddling.
He used to come every week to our house.
He used to bring cheeses from Ciechanow.
They had a dairy factory then, make cheeses.
He would stay in my house, sell the cheeses there, and then in return, he would buy other things.
He would peddle.
So when I came to my brother-in-law, he gave me the money.
That was on a Thursday.
And I was supposed to start going home on a Friday.
Yes, that was the same week.
I was going home on a Friday.
I was going home on a Friday, and he told me he's going home Saturday, and I should go together with him.
It was just a sheer luck.
OK.
